pages.full_text:
sunk for small boats to moor to) until Nov 28th when Antwerp was taken. It survived the June 19th → 23rd storm.
		We ate a delicious Coque Monseiur in the square. I paddled. After visiting the museum again in the afternoon we drove to Bayeux.
		We saw the Bayeux Tapestry, very clearly laid out, Beautiful stitching of English wool. Very inspiring. The English have moustaches + long hair. The hands holding lances or tillers are all correct.
		Bayeux was the first town liberated by the Allies, without a bomb falling - no damage. It's a very attractive town, cobbled streets + a lace museum, that we didn't see. The campsite was supposed to be open, but it wasn't. So we stayed in a hotel with a very mean-looking thin French woman in charge. We ate a good meal at Le Pommier Normande. Simon had sea food salad. I had veg. pâte + tomato sauce. Then we had rabbit. Then cheese - Pont l'Evêque, Lioritot + Camembert, then Coup de Normande.
pages.notes: This page continues from a previous page (text begins mid-sentence). 'Nov 28th when Antwerp was taken' and 'June 19th → 23rd storm' are historical references: the liberation of Antwerp (28 November 1944) and the Great Storm of June 1944 that destroyed the Mulberry harbours — these are background notes about Normandy/WWII history, not dates of the trip. 'Coque Monseiur' is likely 'Croque Monsieur' (a toasted cheese sandwich), spelled phonetically. 'Lioritot' is uncertain — could be a local Norman cheese name; alternative reading not clear. 'Coup de Normande' may be 'Trou Normand' (a Norman tradition of a calvados shot between courses) or a similar local dessert/drink. The ink appears pink/magenta throughout. No handwritten page number visible in the top-left corner. Year cannot be confirmed from this page alone; the broader Normandy trip context (nearby pages mention Wednesday October 9th) suggests possibly 1991 or 2002.

photo_captions: [Bayeux Tapestry — large colour photograph, scene with figures, horses and architectural border]
[Bayeux Tapestry — smaller colour photograph showing Latin inscription: NVN TIL VVILLELMI [?ATHIC] VEN]
pages.notes: Page contains two glued-in colour photographs of the Bayeux Tapestry. The upper image is a large detail showing figures and horses within the characteristic decorative border of the tapestry. The lower image is a smaller detail showing a Latin caption from the tapestry reading 'NVN TIL VVILLELMI [illegible] VEN' (likely 'NUNTII WILLELMI' or similar — partial Latin text from the tapestry). Handwritten page number 49 appears in the top-right corner. The number '284' visible at the top-left of the right image is the mirror of the left-page gutter area (overlap). No handwritten narrative text on this page.
